673b8e846a9f877eb58c9b78.question: Surveys of unmeasured consumption in this country were conducted by Berkeley and Duke following a research agenda set in a 1977 article by Gregory Grossman. Investigations into production “to the left” in this country ended in the deaths of several luxury coat makers and fishery heads. This country experienced a vogue for cybernetics during a decade in which it was mooted as a replacement for a system of “material balances”. In this country, music called “ribs” on (*) X-ray film appealed to the urban youth subculture of “style-hunters”. The 1957 World Festival of Youth and Students began a craze in this country for Levi 501s. Foreign products called fartsa and blat networks of reciprocity were central to the large second economy of this country, which was the largest state that János Kornai termed a “shortage economy”. For 10 points, what country’s command economy ended with perestroika?
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9b78.question_sanitized: Surveys of unmeasured consumption in this country were conducted by Berkeley and Duke following a research agenda set in a 1977 article by Gregory Grossman. Investigations into production "to the left" in this country ended in the deaths of several luxury coat makers and fishery heads. This country experienced a vogue for cybernetics during a decade in which it was mooted as a replacement for a system of "material balances". In this country, music called "ribs" on (*) X-ray film appealed to the urban youth subculture of "style-hunters". The 1957 World Festival of Youth and Students began a craze in this country for Levi 501s. Foreign products called fartsa and blat networks of reciprocity were central to the large second economy of this country, which was the largest state that Janos Kornai termed a "shortage economy". For 10 points, what country's command economy ended with perestroika?
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9b78.answer: Union of Soviet Socialist Republics [or Soviet Union or Soyuz Sovyetskikh Sotsialisticheskikh Respublik; accept Russia; reject “Russian Empire” or “Russian Federation”]
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9b78.answer_sanitized: Union of Soviet Socialist Republics [or Soviet Union or Soyuz Sovyetskikh Sotsialisticheskikh Respublik; accept Russia; reject "Russian Empire" or "Russian Federation"]

673b8e846a9f877eb58c9b86.question: According to a Cuban documentary, this event was backed by the CIA through Operation Pantomime after attempts at bribery failed. The writer of the autobiography Living to Tell the Tale claimed that the 20th century began in his country on the date of this event. An angry mob broke into a drug store to beat a man believed to be involved in this event to death. After this event, Carlos Lleras Restrepo and Mariano Ospina Pérez agreed to reinstate power-sharing through the Unión Nacional. Fidel Castro witnessed the deadly riots after this event because it occurred during the founding of the OAS at the 9th (*) Pan-American Congress. Juan Roa Sierra is commonly identified as the perpetrator of this event, which led to the Bogotazo riots that threatened President Mariano Ospina Pérez. For 10 points, what 1948 killing of a popular Liberal Party leader sparked La Violencia in Colombia?
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9b86.question_sanitized: According to a Cuban documentary, this event was backed by the CIA through Operation Pantomime after attempts at bribery failed. The writer of the autobiography Living to Tell the Tale claimed that the 20th century began in his country on the date of this event. An angry mob broke into a drug store to beat a man believed to be involved in this event to death. After this event, Carlos Lleras Restrepo and Mariano Ospina Perez agreed to reinstate power-sharing through the Union Nacional. Fidel Castro witnessed the deadly riots after this event because it occurred during the founding of the OAS at the 9th (*) Pan-American Congress. Juan Roa Sierra is commonly identified as the perpetrator of this event, which led to the Bogotazo riots that threatened President Mariano Ospina Perez. For 10 points, what 1948 killing of a popular Liberal Party leader sparked La Violencia in Colombia?
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9b86.answer: the assassination of Jorge Eliécer Gaitán Ayala [accept the El Bogotazo until read; prompt on La Violencia and ask “what immediate event sparked it?”]
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9b86.answer_sanitized: the assassination of Jorge Eliecer Gaitan Ayala [accept the El Bogotazo until read; prompt on La Violencia and ask "what immediate event sparked it?"]

673b8e846a9f877eb58c9b8a.question: Description acceptable. During negotiations before this event, a leader proposed the principle of “one taxpayer, one vote” to add voters to the “B” rolls; during those negotiations, two leaders told each other to “think again” in televised statements. The UN's first mandatory economic sanctions were in reaction to this event, which occurred despite a policy abbreviated NIMBAR that had previously blocked attempts by Winston Field. Diplomatic ties severed over this event were repaired 14 years later when the (*) Lancaster House Agreement was signed by Abel Muzorewa. This event occurred a year after former junior partners in the Central African Federation obtained new governments under Kenneth Kaunda and Hastings Banda. This event occurred without the support of Harold Wilson amidst the beginning of the Bush War fought by the ZAPU and ZANU parties. For 10 points, name or describe this decision that created an international pariah state led by Ian Smith.
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9b8a.question_sanitized: Description acceptable. During negotiations before this event, a leader proposed the principle of "one taxpayer, one vote" to add voters to the "B" rolls; during those negotiations, two leaders told each other to "think again" in televised statements. The UN's first mandatory economic sanctions were in reaction to this event, which occurred despite a policy abbreviated NIMBAR that had previously blocked attempts by Winston Field. Diplomatic ties severed over this event were repaired 14 years later when the (*) Lancaster House Agreement was signed by Abel Muzorewa. This event occurred a year after former junior partners in the Central African Federation obtained new governments under Kenneth Kaunda and Hastings Banda. This event occurred without the support of Harold Wilson amidst the beginning of the Bush War fought by the ZAPU and ZANU parties. For 10 points, name or describe this decision that created an international pariah state led by Ian Smith.
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9b8a.answer: Rhodesia’s Unilateral Declaration of Independence [accept descriptions of Rhodesia becoming independent or the creation of Rhodesia; do not accept answers involving Zimbabwe]
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9b8a.answer_sanitized: Rhodesia's Unilateral Declaration of Independence [accept descriptions of Rhodesia becoming independent or the creation of Rhodesia; do not accept answers involving Zimbabwe]

673b8e846a9f877eb58c9b90.question: This city was the base of the Ministry of Reconstruction and Urbanisation, which in the 50s and 60s prioritized so-called “large building” high rises and apartment blocks that became associated with HLM social housing. In the 1990s, most of the national library in this city was moved to a complex of four massive buildings called the Towers of Laws, Time, Numbers, and Letters, respectively. A modernist suburb of this city was converted into a concentration camp after 13,000 Jewish residents were rounded up in a central (*) velodrome. A 1920s plan named after an automobile maker would have gutted this city’s downtown to install 18 cruciform towers. Outgoing heads of state traditionally construct new museums in this city, which in the 1960s underwent renovations under a cultural affairs minister who wrote the novel Man’s Fate. For 10 points, the Montparnasse Tower is the only skyscraper in what city with a business district called La Défense?
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9b90.question_sanitized: This city was the base of the Ministry of Reconstruction and Urbanisation, which in the 50s and 60s prioritized so-called "large building" high rises and apartment blocks that became associated with HLM social housing. In the 1990s, most of the national library in this city was moved to a complex of four massive buildings called the Towers of Laws, Time, Numbers, and Letters, respectively. A modernist suburb of this city was converted into a concentration camp after 13,000 Jewish residents were rounded up in a central (*) velodrome. A 1920s plan named after an automobile maker would have gutted this city's downtown to install 18 cruciform towers. Outgoing heads of state traditionally construct new museums in this city, which in the 1960s underwent renovations under a cultural affairs minister who wrote the novel Man's Fate. For 10 points, the Montparnasse Tower is the only skyscraper in what city with a business district called La Defense?
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9b90.answer: Paris (The first sentence refers to “”. The 1920s plan is Le Corbusier’s Plan Voisin.)
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9b90.answer_sanitized: Paris (The first sentence refers to "". The 1920s plan is Le Corbusier's Plan Voisin.)

673b8e846a9f877eb58c9b91.question: Specific term required. These places were periodically toured by Superintendent Daniel Gookin, who defended their inhabitants in an account of their “Doings and Sufferings”. James Printer was among the residents of these places who helped produce a translation of the Geneva Bible with a new orthography. All but four of these places collapsed after an attack on Swansea and the subsequent internment of their inhabitants on Deer Island. Cutshamekin was among the five leaders who established these places by signing a “covenant of voluntary subjection”. John Eliot’s murdered interpreter John (*) Sassamon belonged to one of these communities at Natick (“NAY-dik”). Suspicion against groups like the Nipmuc led the General Court to disband most of these communities during the war with Metacom. For 10 points, what borderland communities in New England were made up of nominally Christian Native Americans?
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9b91.question_sanitized: Specific term required. These places were periodically toured by Superintendent Daniel Gookin, who defended their inhabitants in an account of their "Doings and Sufferings". James Printer was among the residents of these places who helped produce a translation of the Geneva Bible with a new orthography. All but four of these places collapsed after an attack on Swansea and the subsequent internment of their inhabitants on Deer Island. Cutshamekin was among the five leaders who established these places by signing a "covenant of voluntary subjection". John Eliot's murdered interpreter John (*) Sassamon belonged to one of these communities at Natick ("NAY-dik"). Suspicion against groups like the Nipmuc led the General Court to disband most of these communities during the war with Metacom. For 10 points, what borderland communities in New England were made up of nominally Christian Native Americans?
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9b91.answer: praying towns [or praying villages; accept communities of praying Indians; prompt on Native American, American Indian, Massachusett, Nipmuc, or Pawtucket towns, villages, or settlements] (The Bible is the Eliot Indian Bible. Gookin recorded that only around 10 percent of those who lived in praying towns were baptized Christians.)
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9b91.answer_sanitized: praying towns [or praying villages; accept communities of praying Indians; prompt on Native American, American Indian, Massachusett, Nipmuc, or Pawtucket towns, villages, or settlements] (The Bible is the Eliot Indian Bible. Gookin recorded that only around 10 percent of those who lived in praying towns were baptized Christians.)

673b8e846a9f877eb58c9ba7.question: Three poets from this modern-day country described the first surviving game to use modern rules for the queen in the allegorical poem Chess of Love. A literal queen from this country supposedly pointed out a mate in four to her husband after he blundered away a piece, after which he promptly granted a petition. A medieval text from this country includes rules for Laquet (“lah-KAY”) and the 12 by 12 chess variant Grant Acedrex. Illustrated figures like Edward I play 103 tactical problems in that text commissioned by a king of this country titled The (*) Book of Games. In a 1561 treatise critical of Damiano, a priest from this country described the opening e4, e5, knight f3, knight c6, bishop b5. Chess may have spread in this modern-day country after invasion forces under Tariq ibn Ziyad defeated Roderic and overthrew the Visigothic Kingdom. For 10 points, name this country whose early chess masters include Father Ruy Lopez.
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9ba7.question_sanitized: Three poets from this modern-day country described the first surviving game to use modern rules for the queen in the allegorical poem Chess of Love. A literal queen from this country supposedly pointed out a mate in four to her husband after he blundered away a piece, after which he promptly granted a petition. A medieval text from this country includes rules for Laquet ("lah-KAY") and the 12 by 12 chess variant Grant Acedrex. Illustrated figures like Edward I play 103 tactical problems in that text commissioned by a king of this country titled The (*) Book of Games. In a 1561 treatise critical of Damiano, a priest from this country described the opening e4, e5, knight f3, knight c6, bishop b5. Chess may have spread in this modern-day country after invasion forces under Tariq ibn Ziyad defeated Roderic and overthrew the Visigothic Kingdom. For 10 points, name this country whose early chess masters include Father Ruy Lopez.
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9ba7.answer: Kingdom of Spain [or España; accept historical kingdoms within the borders of Spain like Castille, Aragon, or Valencia] (The second sentence is a Spanish legend concerning Isabella, Ferdinand, and Columbus.)
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9ba7.answer_sanitized: Kingdom of Spain [or Espana; accept historical kingdoms within the borders of Spain like Castille, Aragon, or Valencia] (The second sentence is a Spanish legend concerning Isabella, Ferdinand, and Columbus.)

673b8e846a9f877eb58c9bb5.question: This historian relied on James Heath’s chronicles for a book whose title was borrowed by Franz Leopold Neumann to describe Nazi Germany as a fractured society united only by antipathy toward organized labor. This historian analyzed the papacy as “the ghost of the deceased Roman Empire, sitting crowned upon the grave thereof”. This historian called Peter Lombard and John Duns Scotus “two of the most egregious blockheads in the world” in a passage that criticizes (*) universities for spreading the “obscurity” of Aristotle. Noel Malcolm edited the letters of this historian, whose translation of Thucydides inspired the name of a game theoretic “trap” that encourages pre-emptive strikes. A dialogue between a student and a doctor by this historian blames Papists and Presbyterians for pursuing their own ends instead of peace. For 10 points, name this polymath who described the causes of the English Civil War in Behemoth.
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9bb5.question_sanitized: This historian relied on James Heath's chronicles for a book whose title was borrowed by Franz Leopold Neumann to describe Nazi Germany as a fractured society united only by antipathy toward organized labor. This historian analyzed the papacy as "the ghost of the deceased Roman Empire, sitting crowned upon the grave thereof". This historian called Peter Lombard and John Duns Scotus "two of the most egregious blockheads in the world" in a passage that criticizes (*) universities for spreading the "obscurity" of Aristotle. Noel Malcolm edited the letters of this historian, whose translation of Thucydides inspired the name of a game theoretic "trap" that encourages pre-emptive strikes. A dialogue between a student and a doctor by this historian blames Papists and Presbyterians for pursuing their own ends instead of peace. For 10 points, name this polymath who described the causes of the English Civil War in Behemoth.

673b8e846a9f877eb58c9bcb.question: A paper about this object by Martin-Castro and Ucar calls attention to a note reading “108 or 120” in the corner of a manuscript because the difference corresponds to exactly 15 millimeters on this object. Peter Stadlen spent years looking for this object only to find it damaged, preventing the testing of his theory that 65 of its 135 uses were faulty. The nephew of this object’s owner, Karl, sent info from its use out of context to the sons of Bernard Schott. As an early adopter, the owner of this object obtained it in 1815 after working on (*) Wellington’s Victory with its creator, who copied its design from Dietrich Nikolaus Winkel. An ongoing dispute over the unusually high numbers created with this object, built by Johann Nepomuk Maelzel, are traditionally ignored by performers outside of members of the HIP movement like John Eliot Gardiner. For 10 points, name this object that a composer inspired the historically confusing and unusually fast markings for pieces like the Choral Symphony.
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9bcb.question_sanitized: A paper about this object by Martin-Castro and Ucar calls attention to a note reading "108 or 120" in the corner of a manuscript because the difference corresponds to exactly 15 millimeters on this object. Peter Stadlen spent years looking for this object only to find it damaged, preventing the testing of his theory that 65 of its 135 uses were faulty. The nephew of this object's owner, Karl, sent info from its use out of context to the sons of Bernard Schott. As an early adopter, the owner of this object obtained it in 1815 after working on (*) Wellington's Victory with its creator, who copied its design from Dietrich Nikolaus Winkel. An ongoing dispute over the unusually high numbers created with this object, built by Johann Nepomuk Maelzel, are traditionally ignored by performers outside of members of the HIP movement like John Eliot Gardiner. For 10 points, name this object that a composer inspired the historically confusing and unusually fast markings for pieces like the Choral Symphony.
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9bcb.answer: Ludwig van Beethoven’s metronome [prompt on metronome or Maelzel’s metronome and ask “who owned it?”]
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9bcb.answer_sanitized: Ludwig van Beethoven's metronome [prompt on metronome or Maelzel's metronome and ask "who owned it?"]

673b8e846a9f877eb58c9bd4.question: Translation or original language term acceptable. Extensive cleaning was required when this system was used to facilitate the daimyō-gyōretsu. Using this system was the most common reason an official or noble would use a honjin, in contrast with commoners using hatago. The names of Kansai and Kanto are derived from being west or east of a place used by this system in Hakone (“ha-koh-nay”), which facilitated the elaborate processions of the Sankin-kōtai. During the 17th century, cedar and cryptomeria trees were planted as part of the establishment of this system, whose ceremonial center was the Nihonbashi. (*) Tokugawa Ieyasu established 53 locations called shukuba as part of this system, whose “Central Mountain” and “East Sea” sections are depicted in two different series of ukiyo-e prints by Utagawa Hiroshige. For 10 points, scenic post stations were built to provide food and shelter to users of what transportation system designed to converge in Tokyo?
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9bd4.question_sanitized: Translation or original language term acceptable. Extensive cleaning was required when this system was used to facilitate the daimyo-gyoretsu. Using this system was the most common reason an official or noble would use a honjin, in contrast with commoners using hatago. The names of Kansai and Kanto are derived from being west or east of a place used by this system in Hakone ("ha-koh-nay"), which facilitated the elaborate processions of the Sankin-kotai. During the 17th century, cedar and cryptomeria trees were planted as part of the establishment of this system, whose ceremonial center was the Nihonbashi. (*) Tokugawa Ieyasu established 53 locations called shukuba as part of this system, whose "Central Mountain" and "East Sea" sections are depicted in two different series of ukiyo-e prints by Utagawa Hiroshige. For 10 points, scenic post stations were built to provide food and shelter to users of what transportation system designed to converge in Tokyo?
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9bd4.answer: Gokaidō [or the Five Routes; accept Tōkaidō or Nakasendō or Kōshū Kaidō or Ōshū Kaidō or Nikkō Kaidō; accept East Sea Route or Central Mountain Route until read; prompt on answers describing the Japanese or Tokugawa system of roads or highway; prompt on post stations until mentioned and ask “what larger system were these built around”]
673b8e846a9f877eb58c9bd4.answer_sanitized: Gokaido [or the Five Routes; accept Tokaido or Nakasendo or Koshu Kaido or Oshu Kaido or Nikko Kaido; accept East Sea Route or Central Mountain Route until read; prompt on answers describing the Japanese or Tokugawa system of roads or highway; prompt on post stations until mentioned and ask "what larger system were these built around"]

673b8e856a9f877eb58c9bfa.question: The so-called “Step-Empress” of the Nara clan supposedly lost her status as consort for doing this action to spite the Emperor. An 1876 lawsuit against Sheriff Nunan struck down a California “Sanitary Ordinance” that required all prisoners do this action. The Qianlong emperor personally led a hunt for a society of sorcerers whom he claimed were doing this action to steal their victims’ souls. The marshal Zhāng Xūn was nicknamed for not doing this action, unlike most of the (*) New Army. Lu Xun’s story “Storm in a Teacup” plays on the mass gatherings of this action that accompanied the Xinhai Revolution. The “Six Bad Policies” included adopting qízhuāng clothing and a 10-day ultimatum to do this action pushed by the regent Dorgon. For 10 points, Qing conquerors claimed Han Chinese could “keep their heads” by doing what action, thereby adopting the queue?
673b8e856a9f877eb58c9bfa.question_sanitized: The so-called "Step-Empress" of the Nara clan supposedly lost her status as consort for doing this action to spite the Emperor. An 1876 lawsuit against Sheriff Nunan struck down a California "Sanitary Ordinance" that required all prisoners do this action. The Qianlong emperor personally led a hunt for a society of sorcerers whom he claimed were doing this action to steal their victims' souls. The marshal Zhang Xun was nicknamed for not doing this action, unlike most of the (*) New Army. Lu Xun's story "Storm in a Teacup" plays on the mass gatherings of this action that accompanied the Xinhai Revolution. The "Six Bad Policies" included adopting qizhuang clothing and a 10-day ultimatum to do this action pushed by the regent Dorgon. For 10 points, Qing conquerors claimed Han Chinese could "keep their heads" by doing what action, thereby adopting the queue?
673b8e856a9f877eb58c9bfa.answer: cutting hair [accept descriptions of either cutting queues, biànzi, or braids, shaving one’s forehead, cutting one’s hair short, or changing one’s hairstyle; accept adopting the queue and equivalent descriptions before “queue”]
